---
title: "Methods — joint-4PL vs. two-stage bias simulation"
date: 2026-05-13
date-modified: 2026-05-16
author: "Daniel Noble (with Claude assistance)"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    number-sections: true
    fig-cap-location: top
    df-print: kable
---

::: {.callout-important}
**Updated 2026-05-15.** The previous design was scrapped after two bugs in
the simulation harness were discovered and one structural omission was
added by Daniel:

1. Several `Sweep A` cells in the original sensitivity-sweep design were
   running with infeasible DGPs (`u_T > 1` at the cold end of the design
   grid), producing NaN survival probabilities for ~50% of cups at low
   temperatures. The OLS-truth function had no upper-bound check; the
   simulation kept running.
2. The driver omitted `truth = truth` when calling the dataset generator,
   so every cell — across the main DGP study and all three sweeps — was
   silently generating data from the baseline DGP regardless of CLI
   overrides. ~28 hr of compute was thrown away. See
   `feedback_sim_preflight.md` for the institutional fixes that now
   prevent both classes of bug.
3. The design adds a third estimator — a *patched* two-stage that uses a
   beta-binomial Stage-1 GLM rather than the binomial GLM used by the
   field default. With three estimators per scenario, the contrast
   between classical and patched isolates the *likelihood* cost of the
   field default; the contrast between patched and joint 4PL isolates
   the *shape / architecture* cost.

The redesign below supersedes the original eight-cell design. All results
notes that depended on the buggy outputs (`2026-05-14-two-stage-bias-sim-results.qmd`,
`2026-05-15-sensitivity-sweeps.qmd`) are stale and need to be regenerated
once the corrected simulation completes.
:::

Methods section for a controlled simulation that compares three TDT
estimators side-by-side: the field-default two-stage pipeline (binomial
GLM Stage 1), a patched two-stage pipeline (beta-binomial GLM Stage 1)
that addresses likelihood misspecification without changing the
architecture, and the joint Bayesian 4PL developed in this paper.

## Estimators

All three estimators are fit to every simulated dataset.

### Two-stage (binomial) — the field default

**Stage 1.** For each temperature independently, a binomial GLM with
logit link is fitted to survivor counts as a function of $\log_{10} t$.
The per-temperature $\log_{10} \text{LT}_{50}$ is read off as
$-\hat\beta_0/\hat\beta_1$ with delta-method standard error from the
Stage-1 covariance.

**Stage 2.** An unweighted ordinary least squares regression of the five
$\log_{10} \text{LT}_{50}$ values on temperature gives slope and
intercept estimates from which $z = -1/\hat\beta_1^{(2)}$ and
$\text{CT}_{\max,1\text{h}} = (\log_{10} 60 - \hat\beta_0^{(2)}) /
\hat\beta_1^{(2)}$ follow. The 95% confidence interval comes from the
delta method on the Stage-2 covariance. Both Normal-quantile and
small-sample t-quantile delta-method CIs are reported (see
[CI construction](#ci-construction) below); the simulation records
both as separate "methods" so the cost of the small-sample correction
on coverage is directly observable. A dataset is marked as a two-stage
success only when every temperature delivers a finite, negative-slope
Stage-1 fit and the Stage-2 slope is itself finite and negative.

Unweighted Stage-2 OLS is retained deliberately — it matches the version
of the pipeline most commonly reported in the empirical TDT literature
and is the comparator the reader will be familiar with.

### CI construction: Normal vs t quantile {#ci-construction}

For $z = -1/\hat\beta_1^{(2)}$ and
$\text{CT}_{\max,1\text{h}} = (\log_{10}60 - \hat\beta_0^{(2)})/\hat\beta_1^{(2)}$,
the delta-method standard error follows from the gradient and the
Stage-2 covariance $V_2$. The default field practice constructs CIs as
$\hat\theta \pm z_{0.975}\,\widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat\theta)$ — *Normal*
quantiles. With only $n_T - p$ residual degrees of freedom in Stage 2
(here $5-2 = 3$ at the full design, $3-2 = 1$ at the sparse design),
$z_{0.975} = 1.96$ systematically under-covers because the SE itself is
estimated from limited data.

The *t-quantile* variant constructs CIs as
$\hat\theta \pm t_{0.975,\,n_T-2}\,\widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat\theta)$,
giving $t_{0.975,3} = 3.18$ (full design) and $t_{0.975,1} = 12.71$
(sparse design). The simulation runs both and reports them as separate
methods (`two_stage_bin` / `two_stage_bin_t` and analogously for the BB
variant) so the gap is the observed small-sample-correction effect.

### Two-stage (beta-binomial) — the patched variant

Same two-stage architecture, but Stage 1 swaps the binomial GLM for a
beta-binomial GLM (logit link) fitted via `glmmTMB::glmmTMB(...,
family = betabinomial())`. The dispersion parameter is estimated per
temperature. Stage 2 remains unweighted OLS on the resulting per-T
$\log_{10} \text{LT}_{50}$ estimates.

This variant exists for one reason: to disentangle the *likelihood*
component of the field default's deficit from the *shape / architecture*
component. The contrast between the binomial two-stage and the patched
two-stage isolates the likelihood effect (both have the same two-stage
architecture; only the Stage-1 likelihood differs). The contrast
between the patched two-stage and the joint 4PL isolates the shape /
architecture effect (both have the correct likelihood for the
beta-binomial scenarios; only the architecture differs).

### Joint Bayesian 4PL

The joint model is the estimator developed in this paper. It is a
non-linear `brms` formula with a `beta_binomial(link = "identity")`
family in which all four 4PL sub-parameters — the reparameterised lower
asymptote, upper asymptote, slope, and midpoint — carry a centred-
temperature slope:

$$
p(T, t) = \ell(T) + \frac{u(T) - \ell(T)}{1 + \exp\!\bigl[k(T) \cdot (\log_{10} t - \text{mid}(T))\bigr]}
$$

with $u, \ell, k, \text{mid}$ each modelled as $\beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot
(T - \bar T)$. Default priors from `bayesTLS::make_4pl_priors()` are
used. Samples are drawn with `cmdstanr` (**3 chains × 3,000 iterations,
1,500 discarded as warm-up → 4,500 post-warmup draws**;
`adapt_delta = 0.95`, `max_treedepth = 16`). The previous configuration
(2 chains × 2,000 iter, `max_treedepth = 14`) saturated the tree-depth
ceiling on every post-warmup iteration in 22 of 23 cells; the new
configuration absorbs the boundary-asymptote geometry of Scenario 1 and
gives enough draws to make tail-quantile CI summaries stable.

Post-fit, $z$ and $\text{CT}_{\max,1\text{h}}$ are extracted with
`bayesTLS::extract_tdt(..., lethal = TRUE)` using the rate-multiplier
definition of $T_{\text{crit}}$. The simulation extracts both
`target_surv = "relative"` (default — threshold at the midpoint between
the posterior-estimated asymptotes) and `target_surv = "absolute"`
(threshold at $p = 0.5$ literal) from the same fit, reported as
separate "methods" (`joint_4pl` and `joint_4pl_abs`). The analytical
truth function evaluates absolute-50% LT50 at each design temperature,
so the `joint_4pl_abs` extract is on the same definition as truth; the
`joint_4pl` extract gives the relative-threshold variant for direct
comparison. We report the posterior median and 2.5 % / 97.5 %
quantiles.

## Performance metrics

For each estimator and each output quantity ($z$,
$\text{CT}_{\max,1\text{h}}$), each simulated dataset contributes one row
with signed bias, squared bias (for RMSE aggregation), 95 % CI lower /
upper, coverage indicator, and CI width. Per-cell summaries report:

- Mean signed bias and Monte-Carlo SE of the bias
- RMSE and Monte-Carlo SE
- Empirical 95 % coverage rate and Monte-Carlo SE (binomial proportion)
- Median 95 % CI width

These are reported twice per cell — once for *all fits*, once for *fits
that pass all joint-4PL convergence diagnostics* (see
[Diagnostic checks](#diagnostic-checks)).

### Sample-size adequacy

Monte-Carlo standard error thresholds: 0.05 °C for bias, 0.015 for
coverage. At $N_{\text{sim}} = 1000$ (every scenario) these thresholds
are comfortably met for all method × quantity combinations under the
baseline DGP. The sensitivity sweeps previously ran at
$N_{\text{sim}} = 500$ to halve per-cell compute, but the 2026-05-16
rebuild standardised on $N_{\text{sim}} = 1000$ throughout — the
shorter per-sim wall times after the sampler retuning made the doubled
sweep count cheap. The driver auto-checks both thresholds at every cell
and prints a warning if either is exceeded.

## Truth

For each simulated dataset, the true $z$ and
$\text{CT}_{\max,1\text{h}}$ are computed analytically from the
data-generating curve. The procedure: evaluate the true
$\log_{10} \text{LT}_{50}$ at every design temperature; fit a line
through those values; read off $z = -1 / \text{slope}$ and
$\text{CT}_{\max,1\text{h}} = (\log_{10} 60 - \text{intercept}) /
\text{slope}$. For the baseline shape (only midpoint varying with $T$),
$\log_{10}\text{LT}_{50}(T)$ is exactly linear in $T$ and the procedure
is an analytical identity. For shape-perturbing scenarios, the fitted
line is the linear projection of the (non-linear) true
$\log_{10}\text{LT}_{50}(T)$ curve onto the design grid — which is the
target both estimators converge to, by construction of the OLS at
Stage 2.

The implementation lives in `compute_ols_truth()` in
[`scripts/simulations/sim_functions.R`](../scripts/simulations/sim_functions.R)
and is checked for feasibility before any data is simulated: the
function rejects DGPs whose $u_T$, $\ell_T$, or $k_T$ leave
$0 < \ell < 0.5 < u < 1$ or $k > 0$ at any design temperature.

## Design grid

The default design grid is five assay temperatures
$T \in \{30, 32, 34, 36, 38\}\,^\circ\text{C}$ × six exposure durations
$t \in \{1, 5, 15, 45, 135, 405\}$ minutes — 30 cells per dataset.
Scenario 8 explicitly varies this between the full grid and a sparse
grid (three temperatures $\{30, 34, 38\}\,^\circ\text{C}$ × four
durations $\{1, 15, 135, 405\}$ minutes — 12 cells per dataset).

Each (temperature × duration) cell is replicated by $n_{\text{reps}}$
independent cups (the number varies by scenario). Each cup contains $N$
individuals, $N$ drawn from a discrete uniform on
$\{10, 11, \ldots, 20\}$ — capturing the modest, unbalanced replication
typical of empirical TDT assays. So a Scenario-1 dataset with
$n_{\text{reps}} = 5$ contains $5 \text{ T} \times 6 \text{ durations}
\times 5 \text{ reps} = 150 \text{ cups}$, each with 10–20 individuals,
for a total of roughly 1,500–3,000 individuals per simulated experiment.

## Scenarios

Eight scenarios. Scenarios 1–2 establish equivalence and isolate the
likelihood effect; Scenarios 3–5 introduce qualitatively different
shape perturbations; Scenarios 6–8 are sensitivity sweeps that map the
dose-response of the comparison against parameters varied within a
scenario.

**Truth column notation.** `param ~ 1: value` means the parameter is
constant across temperature at that value. `param ~ 1 + T_c:
(β_0, β_1)` means the parameter equals $\beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot T_c$
where $T_c = T - 34$.

**Default biological parameters** (overridden where the row says so):
$u = 0.92$, $\ell = 0.05$, $k = 8$, $\text{mid}_0 = 1.5$,
$m_{\beta_1} = -0.15$, $\phi = 5$ (beta-binomial dispersion).

::: {.column-screen-inset}
| # | Name | Truth | DGP lik. | TS lik. (Cls / Pch) | 4PL lik. | Replication (cups + N per cup) | n_sims | Why | Prediction |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Strict equivalence baseline | u ~ 1: 0.999<br>ell ~ 1: 0.001<br>k ~ 1: 8<br>mid ~ 1 + T_c: (1.5, −0.15)<br>**true z = 6.67, true CTmax_1hr = 32.15** | Bin | Bin / BB | BB | 5T × 6 durations = **30 cells** per dataset<br>**n_reps ∈ {3, 5}** cups per cell<br>**N ~ U(10, 20)** individuals per cup<br>⇒ 90 or 150 cups, ~1,200 or ~2,000 individuals per dataset<br>**2 simulation cells (n_reps levels)** | 1000 | All three estimators correctly specified (BB-4PL collapses to Bin via φ → ∞). Floor — divergence here would signal a bug. | All 3 unbiased; coverage ≈ 0.95. Cls TS wider CIs (Stage-2 OLS on 5 LD50 points only). Pch TS ≈ Cls (no overdispersion to exploit). 4PL narrowest CIs (joint fit shares info across T). |
| 2 | Likelihood misspec only | Same as Scen 1 | BB (φ=5) | Bin / BB | BB | 5T × 6 durations = **30 cells** per dataset<br>**n_reps ∈ {3, 5}** cups per cell<br>**N ~ U(10, 20)** individuals per cup<br>⇒ 90 or 150 cups<br>**2 simulation cells** | 1000 | Same shape, overdispersion added. Isolates the cost of the classical TS's binomial-likelihood assumption. | All 3 unbiased. Cls TS: wider CIs than Pch (efficiency loss); coverage ~ nominal via Stage-2 OLS residual variance. Pch TS: narrower CIs, calibrated. 4PL: narrowest CIs, calibrated. **Cls vs Pch = pure likelihood cost.** |
| 3 | Heat lowers max survival | u ~ 1 + T_c: (0.92, −0.01)<br>ell ~ 1: 0.05<br>k ~ 1: 8<br>mid ~ 1 + T_c: (1.5, −0.15)<br>**true z = 6.54, true CTmax_1hr = 32.12** | BB (φ=5) | Bin / BB | BB | 5T × 6 durations = **30 cells** per dataset<br>**n_reps ∈ {3, 5}** cups per cell<br>**N ~ U(10, 20)** individuals per cup<br>⇒ 90 or 150 cups<br>**2 simulation cells** | 1000 | TS architectures have no asymptote parameter; heat-induced u-drop must be absorbed into the slope, biasing z. | Cls and Pch TS: both biased (same shape misspec). Cls wider CIs (may accidentally cover); Pch narrower CIs (coverage drops more sharply around the biased point). 4PL: bias ≈ 0, calibrated. **Pch vs 4PL = pure shape / architecture cost.** |
| 4 | Asymptotes compress | u ~ 1 + T_c: (0.92, −0.01)<br>ell ~ 1 + T_c: (0.05, +0.01)<br>k ~ 1: 8<br>mid ~ 1 + T_c: (1.5, −0.15)<br>**true z = 6.66, true CTmax_1hr = 32.09** | BB (φ=5) | Bin / BB | BB | 5T × 6 durations = **30 cells** per dataset<br>**n_reps ∈ {3, 5}** cups per cell<br>**N ~ U(10, 20)** individuals per cup<br>⇒ 90 or 150 cups<br>**2 simulation cells** | 1000 | Both asymptotes move toward each other; true z barely shifts because the log-ratio is nearly preserved. Stresses joint 4PL's u + ell parametrisation more than its midpoint estimation. | Cls and Pch: small bias (smaller than Scen 3 because true z perturbation is smaller). 4PL: bias ≈ 0, calibrated. |
| 5 | Heat sharpens curve | u ~ 1: 0.92<br>ell ~ 1: 0.05<br>k ~ 1 + T_c: (8, +0.25)<br>mid ~ 1 + T_c: (1.5, −0.15)<br>**true z = 6.68, true CTmax_1hr = 32.08** | BB (φ=5) | Bin / BB | BB | 5T × 6 durations = **30 cells** per dataset<br>**n_reps ∈ {3, 5}** cups per cell<br>**N ~ U(10, 20)** individuals per cup<br>⇒ 90 or 150 cups<br>**2 simulation cells** | 1000 | TS Stage-1 GLMs allow slope to vary across T (separate fit per T), but Stage-2 OLS assumes linear log10(LD50)(T). Varying k breaks linearity subtly. | Smallest bias of the shape-perturbing scenarios. Cls and Pch: slight bias, coverage near nominal. 4PL: bias ≈ 0, calibrated. |
| 6 | Sensitivity: strength of u drift | u ~ 1 + T_c: u_0 = 0.92, **β_u ∈ {−0.005, −0.010, −0.015, −0.019}**<br>ell ~ 1: 0.05<br>k ~ 1: 8<br>mid ~ 1 + T_c: (1.5, −0.15)<br>**true z ∈ [6.42, 6.60]**<br>**true CTmax_1hr ∈ [32.11, 32.15]** | BB (φ=5) | Bin / BB | BB | 5T × 6 durations = **30 cells** per dataset<br>**n_reps = 5** cups per cell<br>**N ~ U(10, 20)** individuals per cup<br>⇒ 150 cups, ~2,000 individuals per dataset<br>**4 simulation cells (one per β_u)** | 1000 | Maps the dose-response: how does the TS shape bias scale with the strength of the asymptote drift? | Cls and Pch: bias monotonically increasing in \|β_u\|; coverage drops correspondingly. 4PL: bias ≈ 0 throughout. |
| 7 | Sensitivity: level of upper asymptote | u ~ 1: **u_0 ∈ {0.65, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 0.99}**<br>ell ~ 1: 0.05<br>k ~ 1: 8<br>mid ~ 1 + T_c: (1.5, −0.15)<br>**true z = 6.67 (constant)**<br>**true CTmax_1hr ∈ [31.23, 32.22]** | BB (φ=5) | Bin / BB | BB | 5T × 6 durations = **30 cells** per dataset<br>**n_reps = 5** cups per cell<br>**N ~ U(10, 20)** individuals per cup<br>⇒ 150 cups, ~2,000 individuals per dataset<br>**5 simulation cells (one per u_0)** | 1000 | TS GLM extrapolates to u → 1. Sublethal endpoints (PSII, fertility, knockdown) rarely reach 1. Tests cost of the forced-asymptote assumption. | Cls and Pch: z bias small (constant asymptote bias across T cancels in Stage-2 slope); CTmax_1hr bias grows as u_0 drops below 1. 4PL: bias ≈ 0 throughout. |
| 8 | Sensitivity: design intensity | u ~ 1: 0.92<br>ell ~ 1: 0.05<br>k ~ 1: 8<br>mid ~ 1 + T_c: (1.5, −0.15)<br>**true z = 6.67, true CTmax_1hr = 32.09** | BB (φ=5) | Bin / BB | BB | **Full design** (5T × 6 dur = 30 cells per dataset) **or sparse** (3T × 4 dur = 12 cells per dataset)<br>**n_reps ∈ {1, 3, 5}** cups per cell<br>**N ~ U(10, 20)** individuals per cup<br>⇒ 12 to 150 cups, ~160 to ~2,000 individuals per dataset<br>**6 simulation cells (design × n_reps)** | 1000 | When does each method's overhead pay off? At what budget can a study get away with the two-stage? | All methods: wider CIs as data sparsens. 4PL has smallest inflation (info shared across T). At sparse × 1-rep (12 cups total, 4 unique LD50 points), TS variants may fail or carry severe bias. |
:::

**Totals.** 25 simulation cells, 25,000 simulated datasets, 250,000
estimator fits at the new $N_{\text{sim}} = 1000$ standard. Each
dataset yields 10 method × quantity rows (joint 4PL relative + absolute,
two-stage binomial Normal + t, two-stage beta-binomial Normal + t).
Wall time on the project's 5-worker PSOCK setup with 3 chains × 3000
iter (warmup 1500): approximately 15–17 h end-to-end.

## Diagnostic checks {#diagnostic-checks}

Every joint-4PL fit logs:

| Statistic | Threshold |
|---|---|
| Maximum R̂ across parameters | < 1.01 |
| Minimum bulk ESS | > 400 |
| Minimum tail ESS | > 400 |
| Divergent transitions (post-warmup) | = 0 |
| Tree-depth saturations (post-warmup) | = 0 |
| BFMI (per chain) | > 0.3 |
| Combined `all_pass` flag | all of the above |

Per-cell summary reports: pass rate for each individual criterion, the
overall `all_pass` rate, and median + worst-case of each statistic
across the 500–1000 fits in the cell. Cells with `all_pass` rate
< 95 % are flagged for review.

The bias / coverage summary is reported twice per cell:

- **All fits** — every simulated dataset where the estimator produced
  finite point estimates. Reflects the realistic experience of running
  the recommended pipeline with default sampler settings.
- **Convergent fits only** — restricted to fits where the joint 4PL
  satisfies all diagnostic criteria. If the two summaries diverge,
  pathological fits are biasing the headline numbers and sampler
  settings need to be revisited before the simulation is interpretable.

## Reproducibility

The full set of per-simulation results — point estimates, CIs, all
posterior draws of $z$, $\text{CT}_{\max,1\text{h}}$ and $T_{\text{crit}}$
per dataset, and the full diagnostic row per fit — is stored as resume-
safe per-sim RDS files under
`output/sim_twostage/raw/<scenario_label>/` and rolled up into
`summary_<label>.rds`, `meta_<label>.rds`, `draws_<label>.rds`, and
`diffs_<label>.rds` at the end of each cell.

The runner is
[`scripts/simulations/run_simulations.R`](../scripts/simulations/run_simulations.R);
the data-generating helpers and the three estimator wrappers live in
[`scripts/simulations/sim_functions.R`](../scripts/simulations/sim_functions.R).
The scenarios are a plain R table inside the runner (no shell dispatcher); the
runner runs a pre-flight pass over every selected cell before any compute starts
(see `feedback_sim_preflight.md` for what the pre-flight checks).

Parallelism uses a PSOCK cluster (`parallel::makeCluster` +
`parallel::parLapply`) with five workers; a PSOCK cluster (rather than
`mclapply`) is required because `cmdstanr` launches Stan executables
via `processx`, which is not fork-safe under macOS.
